skillName: Identify and name types of angles
misconceptionSummary: Students confuse obtuse and reflex angles because both are 'bigger than a right angle'.

lines.text: Obtuse ends at 180° — 210° is past a straight line

steps.narration: The student called two hundred and ten degrees obtuse because it is bigger than a right angle. But obtuse ends at one hundred and eighty degrees. Two hundred and ten is past a straight line — that makes it reflex, not obtuse.

visuals.ruleText: 180° is the boundary: obtuse is below it, reflex is above it

steps.narration: One hundred and eighty degrees — a straight line — is the exact boundary between obtuse and reflex. Obtuse angles are those that have not yet reached a straight line. Reflex angles have gone past it.

lines.text: Straight-line check: does it look wider than a flat line?

steps.narration: One hundred and fifty degrees is below one hundred and eighty — obtuse. Two hundred and ten is above — reflex. A quick visual check: does the angle look wider than a flat straight line? If yes, it is reflex.

visuals.label: 180° is the dividing line — below is obtuse, above is reflex

steps.narration: One hundred and fifty degrees is obtuse, two hundred and ten is reflex. Always check the angle against one hundred and eighty degrees — the straight line — before naming it.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: calling 210° obtuse because it is bigger than 90°. Obtuse stops at 180°. Any angle greater than 180° is reflex — it has passed the straight line.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Both obtuse and reflex are bigger than a right angle, but they are separated at 180°. Obtuse is between 90° and 180°. Reflex is between 180° and 360°. The straight line at 180° is the dividing boundary.
